content:
# Numbers Chapter 21 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 173-174 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0128-0129 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `NUM_021_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_021_page_002.jpg`; `NUM_021_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_021_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Defeat of Arad (vv. 1-3)

**1** And the Canaanite, the king of Arad, who dwelt in the Negev, heard that Israel was coming by the way of Atharim, and he fought against Israel and took some of them captive.

**2** And Israel vowed a vow to YHWH and said, "If you will surely give this people into my hand, then I will utterly devote their cities to destruction."

**3** And YHWH listened to the voice of Israel and gave over the Canaanites, and they utterly devoted them and their cities to destruction. And the name of the place was called Hormah.

---

### The Bronze Serpent (vv. 4-9)

**4** And they journeyed from Mount Hor by the way of the Red Sea, to go around the land of Edom. And the soul of the people became impatient on the way.

**5** And the people spoke against God and against Moses, "Why have you brought us up from Egypt to die in the wilderness? For there is no bread and there is no water, and our soul loathes this worthless food."

**6** And YHWH sent among the people the fiery serpents, and they bit the people, and many people of Israel died.

**7** And the people came to Moses and said, "We have sinned, for we have spoken against YHWH and against you. Pray to YHWH that he may take away the serpents from us." And Moses prayed on behalf of the people.

**8** And YHWH said to Moses, "Make for yourself a fiery serpent and set it upon a pole; and it shall be that everyone who is bitten, when he looks at it, shall live."

**9** And Moses made a serpent of bronze and set it upon the pole. And it came to pass that if a serpent had bitten any man, and he looked at the serpent of bronze, he lived.

---

### The Journey to Moab (vv. 10-20)

**10** And the Sons of Israel set out and encamped at Oboth.

**11** And they set out from Oboth and encamped at Iye-abarim, in the wilderness that is before Moab, toward the rising of the sun.

**12** From there they set out and encamped in the Valley of Zered.

**13** From there they set out and encamped on the other side of the Arnon, which is in the wilderness that extends from the border of the Amorites; for the Arnon is the border of Moab, between Moab and the Amorites.

**14** Therefore it is said in the Book of the Wars of YHWH,

> "Waheb in Suphah,
> and the valleys of the Arnon,

**15** > and the slope of the valleys
> that extends to the dwelling of Ar,
> and leans to the border of Moab."

**16** And from there to Beer; that is the well of which YHWH said to Moses, "Gather the people together, and I will give them water."

**17** Then Israel sang this song:

> "Spring up, O well! Sing to it!

**18** > The well that the princes dug,
> that the nobles of the people delved,
> with the scepter, with their staffs."

And from the wilderness to Mattanah,

**19** and from Mattanah to Nahaliel, and from Nahaliel to Bamoth,

**20** and from Bamoth to the valley that is in the field of Moab, at the top of Pisgah, which overlooks the face of the Jeshimon.

---

### The Defeat of Sihon (vv. 21-32)

**21** And Israel sent messengers to Sihon, king of the Amorites, saying,

**22** "Let me pass through your land. We will not turn aside into field or into vineyard. We will not drink the waters of a well. By the King's Highway we will go until we have passed through your border."

**23** But Sihon would not allow Israel to pass through his border. And Sihon gathered all his people together and went out against Israel into the wilderness, and he came to Jahaz and fought against Israel.

**24** And Israel struck him with the edge of the sword and possessed his land from the Arnon to the Jabbok, as far as the Sons of Ammon, for the border of the Sons of Ammon was strong.

**25** And Israel took all these cities, and Israel dwelt in all the cities of the Amorites, in Heshbon and in all its villages.

**26** For Heshbon was the city of Sihon, king of the Amorites, who had fought against the former king of Moab and had taken all his land from his hand, as far as the Arnon.

**27** Therefore the ones who speak in proverbs say,

> "Come to Heshbon!
> Let it be built and established, the city of Sihon!

**28** > For a fire went forth from Heshbon,
> a flame from the city of Sihon.
> It consumed Ar of Moab,
> the lords of the high places of the Arnon.

**29** > Woe to you, O Moab!
> You are undone, O people of Chemosh!
> He has given his sons as fugitives,
> and his daughters into captivity,
> to Sihon, king of the Amorites.

**30** > And we shot at them; Heshbon has perished as far as Dibon.
> And we laid waste as far as Nophah,
> which reaches to Medeba."

**31** And Israel dwelt in the land of the Amorites.

**32** And Moses sent to spy out Jazer, and they captured its villages and dispossessed the Amorites who were there.

---

### The Defeat of Og (vv. 33-35)

**33** And they turned and went up by the way of Bashan. And Og, the king of Bashan, went out against them, he and all his people, to battle at Edrei.

**34** And YHWH said to Moses, "Do not fear him, for into your hand I have given him and all his people and his land. And you shall do to him as you did to Sihon, king of the Amorites, who was dwelling in Heshbon."

**35** And they struck him and his sons and all his people until there was no survivor remaining to him, and they possessed his land.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **v. 1 — "Atharim" (הָאֲתָרִים):** The Leningrad Codex reads הָאֲתָרִים (ha-atarim). The meaning is debated: it may be a proper place name or a common noun meaning "the routes" (from תּוּר, "to spy out"). The LXX (Vaticanus) renders this with a different reading. Retained as a proper name following the MT vocalization.

2. **v. 2 — "utterly devote...to destruction" (וְהַחֲרַמְתִּי):** The Hiphil of חרם with the sense of total devotion to YHWH by destruction. The vow uses the infinitive absolute construction (הַחֲרֵם תַּחֲרִים in the conditional protasis implied). Rendered with doubling per binding conventions.

3. **v. 3 — "Hormah" (חׇרְמָה):** An etiological naming; the place name derives from the root חרם ("to devote to destruction"), thus the name encodes the fulfilled vow of v. 2.

4. **v. 4 — "Red Sea" (יַם־סוּף):** Literally "Sea of Reeds." The Leningrad Codex reads יַם־סוּף. Rendered "Red Sea" following conventional English usage, though "Sea of Reeds" is the literal sense. The LXX renders ἐπὶ θάλασσαν ἐρυθράν.

5. **v. 5 — "worthless food" (הַלֶּחֶם הַקְּלֹקֵל):** The adjective קְלֹקֵל is an intensive form (pilpel) from קלל ("to be light, worthless"). Translated "worthless" to preserve the contemptuous tone.

6. **v. 6 — "fiery serpents" (הַנְּחָשִׁים הַשְּׂרָפִים):** The adjective שָׂרָף means "burning" and likely refers to the burning sensation of the venom. The LXX renders τοὺς ὄφεις τοὺς θανατοῦντας ("the death-dealing serpents"), an interpretive divergence from the MT.

7. **v. 8 — "a fiery serpent" (שָׂרָף):** YHWH's instruction uses שָׂרָף alone (without נָחָשׁ), yet Moses makes a נְחַשׁ נְחֹשֶׁת ("serpent of bronze") in v. 9. The wordplay between נָחָשׁ (serpent) and נְחֹשֶׁת (bronze) is deliberate in the Hebrew.

8. **v. 9 — "serpent of bronze" (נְחַשׁ נְחֹשֶׁת):** The paronomasia (נְחַשׁ / נְחֹשֶׁת) is a notable literary feature. The bronze material is Moses' interpretive choice in fulfilling YHWH's command; the divine instruction said only שָׂרָף.

9. **v. 14 — "Book of the Wars of YHWH" (בְּסֵפֶר מִלְחֲמֹת יהוה):** A lost literary work cited as an authoritative source. The quotation in vv. 14b-15 is syntactically fragmentary in the MT, beginning with אֶת־וָהֵב בְּסוּפָה, which lacks a governing verb.

10. **v. 14 — "Waheb in Suphah" (אֶת־וָהֵב בְּסוּפָה):** The text is notoriously difficult. The Leningrad Codex preserves the reading as given. אֶת may be the accusative marker (implying a lost verb) or the preposition "at/with." Translated as a proper name following standard convention.

11. **v. 17 — "Spring up, O well!" (עֲלִי בְאֵר):** The Song of the Well. The imperative עֲלִי is feminine singular, addressing the well (בְּאֵר, fem.). This is an ancient poem likely predating its narrative context.

12. **v. 18 — "scepter...staffs" (בִּמְחֹקֵק בְּמִשְׁעֲנֹתָם):** מְחֹקֵק can mean "scepter" or "commander's staff" (from חקק, "to inscribe, decree"). The LXX reads ἐν τῇ βασιλείᾳ ("in the kingdom"), diverging significantly.

13. **v. 24 — "for the border of the Sons of Ammon was strong" (כִּי עַז גְּבוּל בְּנֵי עַמּוֹן):** The MT reads עַז ("strong/fortified"), explaining why Israel's conquest stopped at the Ammonite border. The LXX reads Ἰαζήρ, taking it as a place name. The MT reading is followed here.

14. **v. 27 — "the ones who speak in proverbs" (הַמֹּשְׁלִים):** From מָשָׁל, "proverb, parable, taunt-song." The poem in vv. 27-30 is a taunt song celebrating the Amorite conquest of Moab, here repurposed in the Israelite narrative.

15. **v. 29 — "Chemosh" (כְּמוֹשׁ):** The national deity of Moab, well attested in the Mesha Stele (9th c. BCE). The taunt attributes Moab's defeat to the impotence of their god.

16. **v. 30 — "And we shot at them; Heshbon has perished as far as Dibon" (וַנִּירָם אָבַד חֶשְׁבּוֹן עַד־דִּיבֹן):** This verse is textually difficult. The verb וַנִּירָם is read as a first-person plural from ירה ("to shoot"), though the LXX and some traditions read differently. The overall sense describes the destruction from Heshbon to Dibon and beyond.

### Cross-References to LXX (Vaticanus)

- **v. 1:** LXX identifies the Canaanite king as dwelling "by the wilderness" rather than "in the Negev," reflecting a variant Vorlage or interpretive rendering of בַּנֶּגֶב.
- **v. 6:** LXX reads "death-dealing serpents" (θανατοῦντας) for MT "fiery serpents" (שְׂרָפִים), an interpretive divergence.
- **v. 13:** LXX adds clarifying language about the Arnon boundary.
- **v. 18:** LXX diverges significantly in the Song of the Well, reading "in the kingdom" for MT "with the scepter."
- **v. 24:** LXX reads "Jazer" for MT "strong" (עַז), reflecting a different parsing of the consonantal text.
- **v. 30:** LXX presents a substantially different reading of this difficult verse.
- **v. 35:** Both MT and LXX agree on the completeness of the defeat of Og, though LXX adds "and all his people" explicitly in the final clause.

### Structural Observations

Numbers 21 forms a pivotal transition in the book of Numbers. It marks the shift from the wilderness wandering narratives to the conquest narratives in Transjordan. The chapter contains five distinct units: (1) the Hormah victory (vv. 1-3), (2) the bronze serpent episode (vv. 4-9), (3) the travel itinerary with embedded poems (vv. 10-20), (4) the defeat of Sihon with its taunt song (vv. 21-31), and (5) the defeat of Og (vv. 33-35). The three embedded poetic units (vv. 14-15, 17-18, 27-30) are notable for their archaic language and apparent antiquity.
